question: Explain the purpose of bagging (bootstrap aggregating) in the construction of a Random Forest and describe one way it helps to reduce model error.
expectedPoints: Bagging involves training multiple decision trees on different bootstrap samples (random subsets with replacement) of the original training data.
This technique reduces the variance of the overall model by averaging the predictions of numerous high-variance, low-bias trees.
It helps prevent overfitting that is common with individual decision trees.

question: Describe the two fundamental sources of randomness in a Random Forest and explain how each contributes to building a model that is robust to overfitting.
expectedPoints: Bootstrap Sampling: Each tree is trained on a random subset of the data, drawn with replacement. This ensures each tree sees a slightly different dataset, decorrelating the trees and making the ensemble more robust.

Feature Randomness (Random Subspace Method): When splitting a node, the algorithm is restricted to a random subset of the total features. This further decorrelates the trees by preventing the same strong features from dominating every tree's structure.
By combining predictions from many diverse (decorrelated) trees, the model averages out their individual errors and captures the general signal in the data rather than noise.

question: Compare and contrast a single Decision Tree and a Random Forest, covering their model complexity, interpretability, and typical performance. Furthermore, explain the mechanism behind Out-of-Bag (OOB) error estimation and why it is a useful property of Random Forests.
expectedPoints: Complexity: A single decision tree is a simple, non-linear model. A Random Forest is a much more complex ensemble of many trees.

Interpretability: A single tree is highly interpretable (e.g., visualized, rules extracted). A Random Forest is a 'black box' model where interpretation is more difficult, relying on overall feature importance metrics.

Performance: A single tree is prone to high variance and overfitting. A Random Forest typically has superior predictive performance, higher accuracy, and is much more robust to overfitting due to averaging.

OOB Estimation: For each tree, approximately 37% of the training data is not used in its bootstrap sample (the 'out-of-bag' data). This OOB data acts as a natural, internal validation set for that tree. The OOB error is the average error for each data point when it is predicted only by the trees that did not include it in their bootstrap sample.
Usefulness: It provides an unbiased estimate of the generalization error without the need for a separate validation set, making model evaluation computationally efficient and data-efficient.
